title: NIS2 Network Security Monitoring and Anomaly Detection

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(a) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(a) (Risk analysis and information-system security policies) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.18: NIS2 Network Security Monitoring and Anomaly Detection.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.18' running on CIM Network_Traffic data model, firewall logs, IDS/IPS events, DNS logs, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=network` with `sourcetype=pan:traffic` (bytes, src, dest, dest_port, action, app from Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks, Splunkbase 2757) and `index=network sourcetype=cisco:asa` (same-style fields via add-on 1620); `index=ids` with `sourcetype=snort` or `suricata` (signature, severity, src_ip, dest_ip); optional DNS and proxy feeds normalized into Splunk CIM Network_Traffic. Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) supplies correlation and risk workflow on top of those sourcetypes.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (Splunkbase 2757), Splunk Add-on for Cisco ASA (Splunkbase 1620)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count sum(All_Traffic.bytes) as total_bytes dc(All_Traffic.dest_port) as dest_ports
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where NOT All_Traffic.dest_category IN ("internal_server","dns","ntp")
  by All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.action span=1h
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| where dest_ports > 20 OR (action="blocked" AND count > 100) OR total_bytes > 10737418240
| lookup asset_lookup_by_str key AS src OUTPUT category as src_category, priority as src_priority
| eval risk_signal=case(
    dest_ports > 50, "port_scan",
    action="blocked" AND count > 500, "brute_force",
    total_bytes > 10737418240, "data_exfiltration",
    isnull(src_category), "unknown_asset",
    1=1, "anomaly")
| sort - count
| table _time, src, dest, src_category, risk_signal, dest_ports, count, total_bytes, action
description: Flags hour-bucketed network behaviour that looks like wide port sweeps, brute-force blocks, or very large flows against the same peers, using accelerated CIM Network_Traffic summaries plus simple thresholds so unusual sequences stand out without waiting for the ticket queue to notice them.
value: Because regulators and boards care whether protective measures actually run day to day, this gives security leadership a defensible trail that someone is watching the wire for denial-of-service precursors, probing, and east-west oddities—not only that a policy PDF exists on the intranet.
implementation: (1) Install Splunk Enterprise Security (263) and Palo Alto / Cisco ASA add-ons (2757, 1620); confirm firewall and IDS data land in CIM-tagged indexes. (2) Accelerate the Network_Traffic data model (Settings → Data models → Network_Traffic, 30-day summary window typical). (3) Populate `asset_lookup_by_str` so unknown-internal sources are rare. (4) Save the SPL as scheduled alert `UC-22.2.18`, 60-minute window, cron every hour, throttle by `src`+`dest` for 4 hours. (5) Route ES notable events or alert actions to your SOC channel with the `risk_signal` field in the title.
visualization: Table (anomalies), Bar chart (risk signals by type), Map (traffic flows), Timeline (blocked connections), Sankey diagram (source to destination flows).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks

knownFalsePositives:
**Patch Tuesday content pushes** — Distinguish: single destination CDN or WSUS IP, uniform `app` label, aligns with change calendar. Suppress: join `maintenance_windows` lookup on dest and add `where in_window!=1` for 48-hour time-bound exceptions.

**Vulnerability scans from Qualys or Tenable** — Distinguish: fixed scanner subnets, steady timing, signatures mention “scanner” in IDS. Suppress: `lookup scanner_subnets.csv src OUTPUT is_scanner` then `where is_scanner!=1` or exception register ticket ID on the scanner asset.

**Load balancer health probes** — Distinguish: tiny payloads, same src LB VIPs every minute, dest_ports low variety but `count` huge. Suppress: `lookup lb_probe_sources.csv src OUTPUT probe | where probe!=1`.

**Cloud egress via NAT pools** — Distinguish: src is carrier-grade NAT range tied to SaaS breakout, dest is a few SaaS APIs. Suppress: extend `asset_lookup_by_str` with `egress_pool` tag and `where egress_pool!=1` for approved breakout ranges; document approved pairs in your exception register.

**Data centre backup fan-in** — Distinguish: overnight window, known backup VLANs, `total_bytes` spike but `dest` is object storage S3-style. Suppress: filter out backup VLAN list via `nis2_uc2218_noise_allowlist.csv` (src, dest_subnet, hour_range) lookup.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) on the search tier with a role that can run accelerated `tstats` against Network_Traffic and read `index=network` / `index=ids`.
• Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (Splunkbase 2757) and Cisco ASA add-on (Splunkbase 1620) installed on search heads plus indexers or heavy forwarders doing parsing.
• Firewall data ingested with sourcetypes such as `sourcetype=pan:traffic` and `sourcetype=cisco:asa`; IDS events as `sourcetype=snort` or `suricata` inside `index=ids` (adjust names to your deployment).
• Network_Traffic data model acceleration enabled for at least 30 days of history; plan roughly 5–15% of firewall ingest volume for summary storage.
• `asset_lookup_by_str` maintained under Enterprise Security → Content → Assets for tying RFC1918 sources to owning teams.
• RBAC: grant `ess_analyst` or custom role `nis2_netsec_read` rights to `network`, `ids`, and summary indexes; avoid sharing admin tokens.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Point Palo Alto firewall syslog or streaming feeds at Splunk heavy forwarders with `props.conf` from add-on 2757 so fields `src`, `dest`, `action`, `bytes`, `dest_port`, and `app` extract consistently. For Cisco ASA, enable `logging timestamp` and send to the same tier with `sourcetype=cisco:asa` tagging.

For IDS, forward unified alerts with 5-minute **forwarder** checkpoints; typical volume is far smaller than firewall permit logs—keep a dedicated `index=ids` with 90-day hot retention.

In ES, run Content Management → Deploy the Network_Traffic tags (CIM version aligned to your ES release). Validate with a 15-minute exploratory search on `index=network sourcetype=pan:traffic` comparing event counts to the firewall vendor UI (Monitor → Traffic logs) for the same interval.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Use the UC SPL block (saved as `UC-22.2.18`). Understanding the pipeline: `tstats` against `summariesonly` reads accelerated Network_Traffic summaries—if this returns zero while raw firewall events exist, acceleration is off or tags missing. The `where` clause drops known DNS/NTP heavy hitters to reduce baseline noise. Thresholds (`dest_ports > 20`, blocked `count > 100`, `total_bytes > 10GB`) are starting points; tighten per segment after **72 hours** of baseline tuning.

Schedule the alert hourly with earliest `-65m@m` and latest `@m` so each run spans full clock hours. Throttle by `src` and `dest` for **240 minutes**.

Step 3 — Validate
Pick a controlled Qualys scan window: you should see elevated `dest_ports` with `risk_signal` “port_scan” unless your scanner subnet is allow-listed—confirm suppression works. For a known backup job, verify `total_bytes` spikes without alert when the `nis2_uc2218_noise_allowlist.csv` lookup marks that subnet.

Spot-check five random alert rows against the Palo Alto Traffic Monitor at the same `_time` hour; bytes totals should match within ingestion lag (<300 **seconds** typical).

Step 4 — Operationalize
Publish an ES correlation search or native alert feeding a “NIS2 — Network anomaly” dashboard: row of single values for counts by `risk_signal`, timechart of blocked brute-force attempts, and drilldown table keyed on `src`→`dest`.

Add a workflow action opening ServiceNow SecOps with prefilled `risk_signal` and CMDB lookup from `asset_lookup_by_str`.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **Zero tstats results** — open Settings → Data Models → Network_Traffic → Acceleration; verify summary builds at 100%. Check that events carry CIM tags: `index=network | tags output=all | head 1`.
• **All sources “unknown_asset”** — refresh ES asset merge or verify `asset_lookup_by_str` key format matches `src` field.
• **Noise from NAT gateways** — collapse behind NAT using `nat_translation_lookup.csv`; enrich before the `lookup asset_lookup_by_str` stage.
• **IDS skew** — if Snort timestamps drift >60 **seconds** from firewall, align NTP on sensors and forwarders; align `_time` using `eval _time=strptime(...)` only if vendor JSON lacks standard time.
• **Performance** — narrow with `where All_Traffic.action!="allowed"` during DDoS storms to keep search under **30 seconds** completion SLA.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an automatic eye on the data pipes in and out of the company. When traffic looks like someone is knocking on every door, hammering a login, or shoving a huge chunk of data somewhere odd, we raise a clear flag so the security team can act—not months later when an audit asks what we actually watched for.
exclusions: Does not by itself prove that every subnet is monitored end to end, does not replace formal risk assessment paperwork, and does not decide whether your organisation qualifies as an essential or important entity—that remains legal and management judgment outside this detection logic.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.18`, associated dashboard panel export, and timestamped `.csv` / PDF run scheduled into `index=audit_evidence` with owner and review hash recorded for NIS2 network monitoring.

controlTest.positiveScenario: During a red-team port sweep from an unscanned lab subnet, within one hour the saved search raises a row where `risk_signal=port_scan`, the source maps to the lab jump host in the asset lookup, and the event count matches the synthetic sweep in firewall logs for that hour.
controlTest.negativeScenario: For a steady-state week with only routine business traffic and an updated allow list for the vulnerability scanner VLAN, the scheduled search completes with zero results after suppression, and the audit sampler shows matching empty output in `index=audit_evidence` for each nightly run.
